text: ASKILJAN, a town of Sweden, on the l. bank of the Umea, at the junction of its principal headstreams, in the district of Umea, in N lat. 64° 53'.
ASKOE, an island of Denmark, off the N coast of Laaland, in N lat. 54° 54', E long. 11° 30'. Pop. 120.—Also an island of Norway, in the Gjelte-fiord, opposite Bergen, in N lat. 60° 28'.
ASKRIGG, a chapelry and market-town in the p. of Aysgarth, N R. of Yorkshire, 17 m. SW of Richmond, on the river Ure. Area of c. 4,790 acres. Pop. 726.
ASKWITH, a township in Weston p., W R. of Yorkshire. Area 3,180 acres. Pop. 398.
ASLACKBY, a parish in Lincolnshire, 24 m. N of Folkingham. Area 4,810 acres. Pop. 507.

ASLACOE (East and West), two wapentakes in the parts of Lindsey, co. of Lincoln. The former comprises 11 parishes, and has an area of 21,560 acres, with a pop. of 2,927; the latter comprises 9 parishes, with an area of 24,680 acres, and a pop. of 2,565.
ASLACTON, a parish in Norfolkshire, 3 m. WSW of St. Mary Stratton. Area 1,310 acres. Pop. 404. -Also a township in the parish of Whatton, Nottinghamshire. Pop. 424.
ASLAR, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Lower Rhine, circle of Bromenfels, 2 m. NW of Wetzler, on the Dille.
ASLEBEN. See ALSLEBEN.
ASLING, a town of Austria, in Illyria, circle of Laibach, 19 m. SW of Clagenfurt. There are marble quarries in the vicinity.
ASMALOE, an island of Norway, at the entrance of the gulf of Christiania, in N lat. 59° 3′, E long. 11°.
ASMANNSHAUSEN, a town of Nassau, on the Rhine, 1 m. NW of Rudesheim. Pop. 600.
ASNÆS, or Aseso Point, a narrow neck of land on the W coast of the island of Zealand in Denmark.
ASNIERES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, cant. of St.-Jean-d'Angely. Pop. 1,294.—Also a hamlet in the dep. of Cher, near Bourges. Pop. 1,600.
ASNIERES-SUR-OISE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Luzarches. Pop. 1,106.
ASNIERES-SUR-SEINE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine, cant. of Nanterre, on the l. bank of the Seine. Pop. 519.
ASNIK, a town of Great Bokhara, 16 m. E of Balkh.
ASO, a river of Italy, in the States-of-the-Church, rising in Monte Viso, and flowing into the Adriatic near Pedaso.
ASOF. See Azor.
ASOLA, a town of Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, deleg. and 18 m. WNW of Mantua, on the l. bank of the Chiesa. Pop. 4,000. It is fortified, and is a place of great antiquity.
ASOLO, a town of Lombardy, in the deleg. and 19 m. WNW of Treviso. Pop. 3,000.
ASON, a river of Spain, in Asturias, flowing into the bay of Biscay between Laredo and Santona.
ASONA, a river of Italy, in the States-of-the- Church, rising in the Apennines, and flowing into the Adriatic, 7 m. SE of Fermo, after a course of 30 m.
ASONUSA, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, 85 m. N of Jeddo.

ASOPO, a river of Greece—the ancient Asopus— which rises in the Platæan plain, in the nomarchy of Thebes, in the midst of a small marsh [Clarke], and winding round to the r. encloses the land in which the v. of Platana lies; it then flows in an E by N, and ENE direction, separating the ancient Theban plain from that of Platæa, passes Tanagra, now called Graimada, on its l. bank near the confluence of the Thermodon, enters the nomarchy of Attica, and falls into the gulf of Euripus, between Délisi, the ancient Delium, and Oropó, the ancient Oropus. In winter it is a muddy torrent; and for eight months of the year is wholly dry. [Hawkins.] The modern name of the A. at the village of Sycamino, near its mouth, is Borién. Its basin is bounded on the S by a range of mountains of which Mount Elate, the ancient Cithæron, is the western, and Mount Nozia, the ancient Parnes, is the eastern. A high open plain bounds it on the northern side. A narrow flat strip of land fringes the shore of the gulf at the mouth of this river, and forms an important pass from Attica into Beeotia. - Clarke's Travels, vol. vii. - Wordsworth's Athens.—Kiepert's Atlas von Hellas.

ASOPUS, a river of the Morea, in the nomarchy of Corinthia. It rises at the foot of Mount Gavria, a picturesque rugged mountain of a considerable height, flows through the Phliassæan valley in an E by N direction to Phlius, where it turns N and enters Sicyonia, through which it flows in a NNE direction to the bay of Corinth .- Leake.
ASPACH, a town of Bavaria, in the Isar circle, 18 m. NNW of Munich. Pop. 600.—Also a commune of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, cant. of Altkirch. Pop. 551.
ASPACH-LE-BAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, cant. of Cernay. Pop. 624.
ASPALL, a parish in Suffolk, 6 m. S from Eye. Area 1,240 acres. Pop. 132.
ASPANG, a town of Austria, in the circle of Vienna, 18 m. SSW of Neustadt. Pop. 620.

ASPARN-AN-DER-ZAYA, a town of Austria, in the circle of Vienna, 16 m. NNE of Korneuburg, on the Zaya, an affluent of the Morawa. Pop. 1,256. There is a fine old castle here belonging to the counts of Breuner.

ASPAS, or Asupas, a village of Persia, in Farsistan, 78 m. N of Shiraz. During the reign of the Sefi monarchs, it was a flourishing town and military station. Here is the fatal pool or spring in which Baharam Gour was engulphed while hunting. Two serjeants, in the train of Sir John Malcolm's mission, lost their lives in the same place.
ASPATRIA, a parish and township in Cumberland, 6 m. NE of Maryport, on the line of the Maryport and Carlisle railroad. Area of p. 8,610 acres. Pop. 1,921; of township, 988.

ASPE, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 14 m. W of Alicant, near the Taroffa. Pop. 5,700. Beautiful marble is quarried here.—Also a valley of great beauty in the French dep. of the Basses-Pyrénées, extending 27 m. from the foot of Mount Aspe, along a river of the same name, to Oleron, where, by its junction with the Gave d'Ossan, it forms the Gave d'Oleron.

ASPE BAY, an indentation of the gulf coast of Cape Breton, between Cape North and Cape Egmont. It has a harbour for boats, and a fine beach. The pop. on the coast are located round the lagunes formed within the beach, and occupy themselves in farming and fishing.
ASPEDEN, a parish in Herts, 1 m. SW of Buntingford, on a stream called the Rib. Area 1,620 acres. Pop. 529.
ASPELAER, a commune and village of Belgium, in E Flanders, 9 m. SSW of Alort. Pop. 1,040. ASP
ASPER, a commune and village of Belgium, in E Flanders, on the Schelde, 9 m. Sof Ghent. Pop. 1,925.
ASPEREN, a town of Holland, in the prov. of S Holland, 6 m. E by N of Gorkum, on the Linge river. Pop. 1,000.
ASPERG. See Asberg.

ASPERN, or ASPARN, a village of Lower Austria, in the circle of the Lower Manhartsberg, on an arm of the Danube, nearly opposite but a little to the E of Vienna, and N of the island of Lobau. Pop. 900. It is celebrated as having given name to one of the severest contests between France and Austria in the campaign of 1809. On the 12th of May, Napoleon had made himself master of Vienna, and the Archduke Charles took up his position on the l. bank of the Danube, but resolved to suffer a part of the enemy's forces to pass the Danube, and then to surround them with his own army, and drive them if possible into the river. Every thing seemed to favour this plan; but it was frustrated by the energy of the French general, and the extraordinary valour of his troops. The archduke had stationed himself behind Gerasdorf, between Bisamberg and Russbach, from which he issued with his army in five columns, consisting of 75,000 men, with 288 pieces of cannon, May 21st, at noon, just as Napoleon, with about half his forces, had left the island of Lobau in the Danube. By a dexterous evolution of his troops, the archduke immediately formed a semi-circle, in which the French army was, in a manner, enclosed; and in the narrow plain between Aspern and Esslingen, a village about 1 m. to the E, a bloody engagement commenced. Every thing depended on the possession of these two villages, which were at first in possession of the French: Aspern was taken by the Austrians, again lost and retaken, till they, at length, remained masters of it: from Esslingen they were continually repulsed. Napoleon repeatedly attempted to force the centre of the Austrians, but was frustrated by the firmness of their infantry. At last, the darkness of the night put a temporary stop to the tremendous contest. The bridge, connecting the right bank of the Danube with the S side of the island of Lobau, had already been destroyed, so that the French reinforcements came up slowly, being compelled to sail over in small parties, and the whole corps of Davoust were idle spectators of the battle from that bank. Notwithstanding these disadvantages, the battle was renewed on the 22d; the French being now at least equal to the Austrians in number. The engagement was of the same exterminating character as that of the day before. Thousands of lives were sacrificed in vain attempts to capture the villages. Aspern continued to be the strong-hold of the Austrians, and Esslingen of the French. When Napoleon gave up all hopes of gaining the victory by forcing the centre of the Austrians, Esslingen served to secure his retreat to the island of Lobau, which was disturbed only by the cannon of the archduke. It has been said, that the archduke did not make a proper use of his advantage; but the truth of this assertion is by no means clear, if we consider the position of the French on the island, the courage and skill of his adversary, and the want of materials for rebuilding bridges. The loss of the Austrians in killed and wounded was estimated at a third of the whole army; that of the French at 30,000. The latter lost, on this occasion, Marshal Lannes, and Generals D'Espagne, St. Hilaire, and Albuquerque. The feelings of the combatants were too violent to allow of many prisoners being made.
ASPET, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, arrond. of Saint-Gaudens. The cant. comprises 20 com. The com. is 6 m. SSE of Saint-Gaudens. Pop. 3,693.
ASPHALTITES. See DEAD SEA.
ASPIAS, a village of Portugal, in Alemtejo, on the l. bank of the San Pedro, 5 m. SE of Moura.
ASPIRAU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Herault, cant. of Clermont. Pop. 1,614.
ASPEROSA (CAPE), the SW point of the gulf of Contessa, in Romania; in N lat. 40° 58', E long. 24° 50′.
ASPLANDS ISLANDS, a group belonging to the New South Shetland isles, in S lat. 62° 5', W long. 57°.
ASPLEY, a township in Eccleshall p., Staffordshire. Area 540 acres. Pop. 24.—Also a hamlet in Wootton-Waven p., Warwickshire.
ASPLEY-GUISE, a parish in Bedfordshire, 2 m. N by W of Woburn. Area 1,960 acres. Pop. 1,139.

ASPOE, an island of Russia, in the gulf of Finland, in N lat. 60° 17′ 32″, E long. 27° 8′ 30″. It has a tolerable harbour.—Also an island on the W coast of Norway, in the mouth of the Dale-fiord, in N lat. 61° 10'.—Also an islet of Sweden, 2 m. SW of Carlscrona.
ASPOTOGAN, a lofty peninsular headland on the coast of Nova Scotia, separating Mahon bay from that of St. Margaret.
ASPRA, a town in the States-of-the-Church, 10 m. WSW of Rieti.
ASPRASPITI, a port of Greece, on the N coast of the gulf of Lepanto, 19 m. SW of Livadia.
ASPRES-LES-VEYNES, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Alpes, arrond. of Gap. The cant. comprises 9 com. The com. is 17 m. WSW of Gap. Pop. 744. There are mineral springs here.

ASPRIERES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, arrond. of Villefranche. The cant. comprises 9 com. The com. and town are 14 m. NNE of Villefranche. Pop. 1,443. Zinc and argentiferous lead are mined here.

ASPROMONTE, a commune and town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 6 m. N of Nice.—Also a mountain of Greece, in the Morea, on the E shore of the gulf of Kolokythia.—Also one of the highest mountains in Calabria, rising immediately behind the town of Oppido. It was the Saltus Rheginus, and Rheginus Vertex of the ancients.
ASPRONISI, a deserted rocky island in the Archipelago, near the W coast of Santorini or Thera. It is the Hiera of Pliny.

ASPROPOTAMOS, or ASPROPOTAMO, [i. e., 'White river,'] a river of Turkey and Greece, the ancient Achelons, which rises in the Pindus range, in Mount Codjaca, 24 m. E of Joannina, near the sources of the Arta, and flows S through a mountainous district in which its basin is separated on the W from that of the Arta by the mountains of Agnanda, and Tzumerka, and entering the plains of Acarnania, turns SSW, and flows by two channels into the Ionian sea at Trigardon, near the Echinades islands, after a course of about 140 m. Its tributaries on the l. are the Baba, the Venternico, the Argolianos, and the Tchermissi; on the r. the Seliveno, the Vornikovo and Valtos, and the Aetos. It carries down a great quantity of alluvial matter in its waters; and Urquhart says its charged waters whiten the sea around the Curzolero islands, or Echinades, and render it daily more shallow: whence its modern name. Herodotus notices this fact, and compares the increase of the Egyptian Delta with the effects produced by the deposits of the A.; but Urquhart says that the delta of the A. has evidently increased little in modern times.
ASPULL, a township in the p. of Wigan, in Lancashire, 3 m. NE of Wigan. Area 1,930 acres. Pop. 2,772. Coal is extensively mined here.
ASSA, a valley of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisais, in the Lower Engadine, remarkable for its ASS intermittent spring.-Also a village in the island of Cephalonia, 16 m. NNW of Cephalonia.
ASSAB. See ASAB.

ASSAHAN, a district on the NE coast of Sumatra, intersected by a river of the same name, on which its cap. is situated. Tin is abundant in the interior; and it exports rice, rattans, wax, horses, and dyewoods. The pop. is said to amount to 70,000.
ASSAL. See ASAL.

ASSAM, a country of India, on the W side of the Ganges, occupying the entire valley of the Brahmaputra from Goyalpara in N lat. 26° 10', and E long. 90° 30', to Sudiya in N lat. 27° 50', and E long 95° 45'. It stretches from Goyalpara, with the river in a NE direction; and is enclosed on either side by hills, the lower ranges of which are connected with the Himalaya mountains on the N, which separate it from Butan and Tibet, and the Garrow, Khassia and Jyntea ridges, which divide it from the Birman and Munipur territories on the S. This long strip of well-watered, fertile country, is divided into three portions; namely, Lower Assam, Upper Assam, and the Sudiya country. Lower Assam extends from Goyalpara, on the SW extremity, to the junction of the Dhunsiri river with the Brahmaputra, above the town of Bishanath. Upper Assam extends from the Dhunsiri to the plains of Sudiya; and this last is described by Lieut. Wilcox as a spacious level plain terminating the valley of Assam, intersected by a number of rivers, and surrounded by mountains covered with forest. The first two divisions formerly constituted the kingdom of Assam Proper; and the last was occupied by various tribes, who acknowledged the Assamese supremacy. Its total length from SW to NE is about 360 m. Its breadth varies from 20 to 70 m.; but if measured from the crest of the Himalaya to that of the mountain-boundary on the S, its breadth is much greater; and its area, according to this latter admeasurement, would probably exceed 70,000 sq. m. The higher mountain-districts, however, are occupied on both sides of the country by independent tribes; and the received estimate of the superficies of the Assam territories is as follows: The mountains which form the southern boundary of A. increase in height as they proceed eastwards, attaining, near the head of the Brahmaputra, an elevation of probably 13,000 or 14,000 ft. The Garrows, at the extreme W, rarely exceed 6,000 ft. In the northern or Himalaya range, Chamalari attains an alt. of 25,000 or 26,000 ft.; but towards the sources of the Brahmaputra the Himalaya decreases in elevation. Rivers.] The Brahmaputra in the upper part of its course is called the Lohit, and descends in two head-streams from the high mountain-regions of Tibet, in which all the large rivers which water the Extra-Gangetic peninsula have their sources. It enters the great plain of A. between 96° and 97° E long.; and its general course through this plain is due W to the confluence of the Dihong, which joins it from the N about 20 m. below Sudiya. The plain of Sudiya is about 120 m. in length, and from 40 m. to 60 m. in breadth; and the larger portion of it lies to the S of the Brahmaputra. During a great part of its course through Upper A., the Brahmaputra is divided into two branches, which, re-uniting, form an island called Magholi, 20 m. in length, and from 4 m. to 5 m. in breadth, formerly well-cultivated and inhabited, but now a wilderness. The Dikho, coming from the SE, joins the southern and larger branch of the river, near the lower extremity of this island. Below Magholi the course of the Brahmaputra is W by S for upwards of 80 m., when it again divides and encloses between its bifurcations a very large tract of country. At Goyalpara it enters Bengal. The Dihong, from the N, joins the Brahmaputra in N lat. 7° 45', E long. 95° 25'. Its volume of water is three times as great as that of the Brahmaputra at Sudiya, and was estimated by Captain Bedford, in December 1825, as amounting to 56,564 cubic ft. per second, exclusive of that which is contributed by the Dibong. A little above its confluence with the main stream, the Dihong is joined by the Dibong, whose volume of water Captain Bedford estimated at 19,058 cubic ft. per second. The tributary rivers of the Brahmaputra, within the A. territories, are very numerous, and there is perhaps not a spot of habited ground so situated as to be more than a convenient distance from some navigable stream. General aspect and soil.] The valley itself is a perfect flat, studded with little green conical hills rising abruptly from the level plains to the height of from 200 to 700 ft. Of course it is at all times a swampy region; and seven-eighths of its surface are covered with jungle, emitting febrile miasmata; but in the dry season it is amply susceptible of cultivavation, and produces rich and abundant crops. The soil upon the hills is a rich red loam, with a sprinkling of particles of quartz or of talc. Large masses of granite are scattered over the hills. Productions. The animal and vegetable productions are nearly the same as those of Bengal. Wheat, barley, and millet, are grown, but only in small quantities; transplanted rice, of inferior quality, forms three-fourths of the grain crop. The sugarcane is cultivated in almost every village, and the canes are crushed between coarsely-made vertical cylinders worked by half-a-dozen men, or a buffalo. Cotton is largely cultivated by the hill-tribes; and three varieties of silk are reared. Opium is grown to a considerable extent; and indigo is cultivated in small quantities. Gum-copal is obtained in the Naga hills, and other valuable gums abound. Coffee grows indigenously; and the true tea-plant thrives as favourably upon the mountains of the Kangtis and Singphos, as in the adjoining provinces of China. Mr. Bruce, describing the geography of the tea-producing districts in A., says: "It should be observed that south from Sudiya to the Debree river is generally termed Chyquah; south of Debree river is called the Muttuck country, as far as the Burro- Dehing river. South of the Burro-Dehing river is situated Rajah Purundah Sing's country. From the hill and river called Jowrah Poong, which is nearly at the source of the Debree river, to a place called Beesa, S of the above hill, on the Burro-Dehing river, near the little river Juglow Pauny, is called the Singpho country, being all to the E of the above line. Now it will be perceived, that not a single teatract has been discovered N of the Debree river, and they are all on the S side of it. The Muttuck country, which I traversed most, appears to be one vast tea-country, and I feel confident that not one half of its tea-tracts have been yet discovered. The whole of the soil of the Muttuck country appears welladapted for tea. The tea-tracts in the Singpho country are much larger than those in the Muttuck. All the Singpho territories are overrun with woodjungle, and if only the underwood was cleared, they would make a noble tea-country. The soil is welladapted for the plant. The tea is said to grow on the top of the Nagah hills." Mr. Griffith is of opinion that the tea-plant may be successfully cultivated in A. for the following reasons:-1. That the plant is indigenous to, and distributed extensively over, large portions of Upper A. 2. That there is a similarity of configuration between the valley of A., and two of the best known tea-provinces in China. 3. That there is a similarity between the climate of the two countries, both with regard to temperature and humidity. 4. That there is a precise similarity between the stations of the tea-plant in A., and its stations in those parts of the provinces of China which have been traversed by Europeans. And 5. That there is a similarity both in the associated and general vegetation of A. and the Chinese tea provinces situated about the same latitude. It appears, however, that while A. has the advantage over Kumaon in the facility for export, the latter province will be found to yield a superior quality, almost sufficient to counterbalance the inconvenience as far as foreign markets are concerned. The fact seems to be that the climate of Kumaon is more adapted to the habits of the tea-plant, which requires four or five months wintering before its leaves are perfectly mellowed. The gross tea-crop of A. in 1847 was 1,600,000 lbs.; of 1848, 2,190,000 lbs. The zoology of A. is similar to that of most jungly countries in the East. Wild elephants are numerous, and move in large herds. From 700 to 1,000 of these creatures are annually exported from A. The rhinoceros inhabits the denser and more retired parts of the country. Tigers, leopards, and bears are numerous; and wild buffaloes are found in all parts of the country. The horses are brought from Bostan, and the sheep from Bengal. Snakes are numerous, but few are venomous. Alligators swarm in the Brahmaputra; and white ants are more destructive here than in any other part of India. Among the mineral products of A. are gold, silver, and iron. The smelting of iron has always been a chief branch of industry amongst the hill-tribes. Coal, both lignite and sulphurous, has been found in several places, and petroleum. Amber-mines are said to exist in the prov. of Hukung; and Munkung, in the country of the Birmah Singphos, is celebrated for its precious stones. The gold with which the market of Sudiya is supplied is furnished to some extent by the beds of the neighbouring rivers, and even by that of the Brahmaputra itself. This precious metal, however, is found still more abundantly in the native regions of the Singphos, which are under the influence of Ava. It is obtained in lumps of two and three sicca weight in the streams about Mogawam or Mung- Kum. This region is rich also in other valuable products, and particularly in emeralds and other precious stones. They are found on the side of certain hills about five days' journey from Mung-Kum, within a district of three days' extent, where they are obtained by sinking pits through the alluvial deposits down to the rocky surface. They appear to be found in large masses, which it requires some contrivance and labour to quarry. Climate.] A. has a tropical climate, distinguished by the abundance of the rains, and the unbroken continuance thereafter of dry weather. It is quite uninfluenced by the monsoons. The wind blows from E or NE for nine months in the year; and seldom from any other direction for more than a few days at a time. The hot weather is more moderate and endurable than throughout India generally; and the nights are cool and refreshing. From October to February the sky is clear, and the weather steady. The rains commence in April; the heavy rains set in about the 15th of June; and by the 1st of July, the whole country is an inland sea, the average rise of the Brahmaputra being 30 ft. At this season, the flood of Deucalion is in a great measure realized. The tiger and buffalo swim together in amity; and the elephant and her young, with the wild hog and her sucklings. The native anchors his boat to his own roof-tree; and drags his net in his tobacco garden. The sites of the villages are only known by their roofs appearing above the stream; and all inhabited places would be isolated, but for their connection by causeways 8 ft. high, and 18 ft. broad, the monuments of ancient industry in this region. One of the most splendid of these causeways extends along the whole northern border of the country from Sudiya to Behar. Manufactures.] The Assamese are not a manufacturing people. Their artizans are mostly from India; and even their trade with Bengal, Butan, Tibet, China, and Ava, is engrossed by the Marwaris. Coarse silk is exported; and salt and cotton-goods are the chief imports. The intercourse between A. and Bengal is almost entirely maintained by water. There is free communication between the Brahmaputra and the Ganges, by different inosculations, for boats of the largest size; there are also three landroutes; but two of these are quite impracticable during the rains. There are also routes to Butan, Tibet, Birmah, and China. The Chinese merchants who trade with the Singphos for precious stones and ivory come from the province of Manway, which lies to the E of a range of mountains having the Singpho or Shan tribes immediately on the W. The Chinese merchants cross those mountains, bringing their goods on the backs of mules, and then find a water-passage open to them down the Irawadi until they reach the junction of the river on which Mung-Kum stands, which it takes them five days to ascend. These merchants bring to Mung-Kum, for the supply of the surrounding countries, clothes of various sorts, but particularly broad cloth and nankeen, silks, boxes of various kinds, tea, earthen-ware, lead-plates, copper and silver. Indeed silver is their chief article of import; and some considerable part of what they import to Mung-Kum finds its way to Sudiya. It comes in small lumps, with a Chinese stamp and character on them. The lumps are of different sizes and weights; and appear to be run into small holes formed carelessly with the finger, and then stamped whilst the surface is still soft. Some part of the silver of Sudiya is received from a mine in the Burkampti country to the SE of the station, which is wrought by the Khunungs, a rude mountain-tribe. The route from A. to Ava passes from the new to the old settlements of the Singphos. It is usually described, indeed, as commencing at Rungpore, at the SE extremity of Upper Assam. From that point it takes a NE direction, skirting the whole of the southern boundary of the country of the Bursinaputi, nearly to the place where the Buri-Dihing throws off the Noa-Dihing. In this neighbourhood is New Beesa, which is subject to British supremacy; and a little to the E of S from it lies Old Beesa, where the intervening hills end, and whence the route passes through a fine populous cultivated country, eight days' march to Mogam or Mungkum, from which there is an open road as well as river-navigation to the capital of Ava. The distance between New and Old Beesa is ten days' march, when the march is from dawn to noon. Population.] The Assamese are chiefly of Hindu extraction, but a considerable proportion are Mussulmans, who are, however, held in great contempt. A large mass of the pop. is composed of tribes from the hills, who intermarry with the Assamese. The latter are a weak and degenerate race; they are idle, timid, and intemperate, fond of drinking arrack and of chewing opium. They have high cheek-bones, and a physiognomy resembling the Chinese. The women are fair, and many would be reckoned beautiful in any part of the world, their forms and features morality is a rare virtue amongst them. At Goyalpara there is a small society of native Christians, a remnant of the posterity of some Portuguese soldiers once entertained by the nawab of Dacca. A small colony of Burmese soldiers who surrendered to the British during the war, is settled at Sinigmari, in the Govalpara district; and some Marwari traders, called Kyahs, an interesting class, reside in the principal towns of A. The whole produce of the country passes through their hands; they are wealthy, and live in a style of comfort and even elegance.—Education is at a very low ebb throughout A., and is confined entirely to the males. No man would marry a girl who could read or write; but a thriving English school, attended by upwards of 100 boys, under a European master appointed by government, is now in full operation at Gohatti. Slavery still exists in the rajah's portion of A. All the drudgery of the household, and the labours of the field, are performed by slaves. Many of these slaves are natives who have mortgaged or sold themselves and descendants for a few rupees. The houses of the Assamese are mostly raised on terraces of clay, 3 or 4 feet high. The walls are made of large trees roughly hewn, sunk about 6 feet into the ground, and covered with mats and reeds, sometimes plastered with clay. Many of the European houses are built of mat and bamboo. Carriages and beasts of burden are seldom seen. Where water-carriage-which is the general mode of conveyance—cannot be had, porters are employed. The NE district of A. is occupied by two tribes of the great Shan family,—the Khamtis and the Shingphos or Sinkfos. The dialects of these tribes differ little from that of the Siamese and Burmese, and the characters in use amongst them are essentially the same. The Shans are a finer and more intelligent people than the Burmese, and ten times more numerous, for their kindred races extend throughout the whole country in which all the mighty rivers from the Brahmaputra to the Yung-Kian, or river of Nankin, originate. They occupy entirely the two frontierprovinces of the Burman empire, -Hukum, and Mung- Kum; and all the eastern bank of the Irawadi; they stretch down the Salueen, to Tenasserim; and Laos, Siam, and Cochin-China are their proper countries; they compose half the population of Yunan, a great proportion of that of Sechwen, and stretch up into that district which has always baffled the Chinese, between Tibet, Tartary and Sechwen; whilst A. is chiefly populated by the overpourings of this great people. The Kacharese are Shans; and the Ahoms, the governing race of Upper A. for many centuries, are a tribe from the highest eastern sources of the Irawadi. According to Captain Neufville, [Asiatic Researches, vol. xvi.] the Singphos are divided into twelve classes which are named after their respective chiefs or gams; and the term 'the Twelve chiefs' is used to designate the whole race collectively. The Beesa-gam, Duffagam, and Lutteao-gam, are the most influential of their race; but they have no acknowledged supremacy over the rest: and, indeed, but little union exists between the different clans although they sometimes combine for particular purposes. The Singphos who have emigrated into A. retain the distinctions of their original state, and designate their new settlements by the names of those from which they came; and thus we find the same names of places on the borders of A. and, on the other side of the intervening mountains, on the borders of Ava. The Kakus, intermingled with the Singphos, are divided into four different tribes. The Singphos of A. have mixed up many of the superstitions of their neighbours with the closely approaching the European. Unfortunately religion of Gadama, who nevertheless has a temple and a priest in each of their principal villages. They practise polygamy without restraint, but hold infanticide in abhorrence. The Miris occupy a strip of alluvial land along the N bank of the Brahmaputra, from the island of Majuli to the Dihong. On the N of them are the Abors, who inhabit the southern expanse of the Himalayan chain. The appellation Abors is given to a number of small tribes of one origin, and the same language and customs, who are not however united by a common government. They occupy the mountains immediately to the N and NW of the plain of Sudiya, The term Abor, as Lieut. Wilcox observes, is that which the Assamese has applied to them, and is not used by the people themselves. Its signification is 'Independent;' and, as might be supposed, it is given to such tribes as have not been brought into subjection. The name by which they designate themselves is Padam. Each tribe appears to have a democratic government of its own, and all its proceedings are by the voice of the people met in open council. They eat the flesh of the elephant, rhinoceros, hog, buffalo, kid, and deer, as well as fowls and ducks; but entertain an abhorrence of feeders on beef. The wild animals they shoot by means of poisoned arrows. Their dress is sufficiently slight; and of its use for decency's sake they appear perfectly careless. They have a sort of dhootes made of the bark of the udal tree, which they tie about their loins, or use as a mat to sit on, and for a pillow at night. They sometimes wear basket or cane caps; and they have nearly all "some article of woollen dress, varying from a rudely-made blanket waistcoat to a comfortable and tolerably wellshaped cloak." They pay a kind of worship to a mountain-deity. The Bor-Abors appear to be merely a superior tribe of the same scattered family. They live in the higher ranges of mountains more distant from A.; and are both more powerful and in a somewhat higher state of civilization than the Abors generally. The tribes of the Nagas are very numerous along the whole mountain-ridge between the Sylhet plains and A. They are the most barbarous of all the hill-tribes; and go literally naked. 1830. History.] The earliest historical names connected with this country strongly resemble the Chinese. From the beginning of the 17th cent, the Brahminical doctrines appear to have gradually gained ground in A.; and since the middle of that cent. the governing party have adopted the language of Bengal. Hitherto the Assamese had been a warlike and enterprising people, and every attempt to conquer their country had signally failed; but after their conversion by the Brahmins, the nation sunk into an abject state of pusillanimity, and a scene of internal turbulence and confusion ensued. In 1793 the British power restored the dethroned king of A. to his government; but shortly afterwards Bura-Gohaing, one of the hereditary counsellors of state, assumed the supreme authority. Chunder-Khant, having been excluded from his succession to the throne, procured assistance from the Burmese, and expelled the usurper; but in June 1822, the commander-inchief of the Burmese forces was proclaimed rajah of A., subordinate to the king of Ava. A rupture speedily took place between the new power and the British authorities, whereupon a British detachment entered A., and, in the course of 1825, expelled the Burmese, and attained complete possession of the country, which is now an integral portion of the British empire in India. Upper A., with the exception of the Sudiya district, was given up to the Raja Poorunder Singh, in 1833. He pays a tribute of 50,000 rupees to the English government. Authorities. Pemberton's Report on the E Frontier of British India. - M'Cosh's Topography of Assam. Calcutta, 1837.—Asiatic Researches.—Asiatic Journal.
ASSA-NAGGA, a watering-place, or caravan-station, in the desert of Nubia, on the route between Berber and Assuan, 96 m. N of Goos, in N lat. 19° 30'.
ASSARLO, a town of European Turkey, 28 m. NW of Adrianople, on the l. bank of the Maritza.
ASSAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Herault, cant. of Aniane. Pop. 257.

ASSAYE, or Assye, a village of Hindostan, 28 m. N of Jaulna, in the prov. of Bahar, in the neighbourhood of which, on the 23d Sept., 1803, Wellington, ASS then General Wellesley, at the head of 2,000 European, and 2,500 native soldiers, defeated the combined force of Row Scindia, and the Nagpur rajah, amounting to 30,000 men. When attacked, Scindia's force was drawn up in two lines parallel to the rivers Kailna and Jouah, the left of their second line resting on the village of A., which is only a short distance from the Jouah. Upwards of 1,500 of the small British force were either killed or wounded in this engagement. The confederates lost 1,200 men, and 98 pieces of artillery.
ASSCHE, a canton and town of Belgium, in the prov. of S Brabant, 8 m. N of Brussels. Pop. 5,200. It has a considerable trade in hops, corn, and flax.

ASSE, a river of France, in the dep. of Basses- Alpes, flowing into the Durance on the l. bank, after a course of 45 m. from E to W, in which it passes Senez, Bareme, and Mezd.—Also a branch of the Lippe, in Westphalia.
ASSE-LE-BOISNE, a commune of France, dep. of Sarthe, cant. of Fresnay-sur-Sarthe. Pop. 1,872.
ASSE-LE-RIBOUL, a commune in the dep. of Sarthe, cant. of Beaumont-sur-Sarthe. Pop. 1,464.
ASSEL (WADI), a river of Palestine flowing W into the southern part of the Dead sea.—Also a river of Algeria, flowing into the Zenati. The name signifies 'River of honey.'
ASSELU, a town of Persia, in Laristan, at the embouchure of the Nabon, on the Persian gulf, 66 m. W of Lar.
ASSEMINI, a parish and v. of Sardinia, 6 m. NNW of Cagliari. Pop. 1,467. The inhabitants are chiefly supported by fishing, and the cultivation of oranges.

ASSEM-KALASI, a village of Turkey, in Anadoli, in the sanjak of Menteshe, 12 m. W of Melasso, at the head of a gulf of the same name, and also bearing the name of Alangok-liman, an inlet of the gulf of Mandaliyah.

ASSEN, a town of Holland, the cap. of the prov. of Drenthe, on the Hoorn-diep, and communicating with the Zuyder Zee by a canal 30 m. in length. It is 20 m. S of Groningen. Pop. in 1796, 600; in 1825,
ASSENDELFT, a village of Holland, in the prov. of N Holland, 7 m. NNE of Haarlem, on the Y. Pop. 2,300.
ASSENEDE, a town of Belgium, in E Flanders, 12 m. NNE of Ghent. Pop. 4,000.
ASSENHEIM, a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, on the Nidda, 13 m. NE of Frankfort-on-the-Mayne. Pop. 800. The count of Solms-Rodelheim has a castle here. Coal is wrought in the vicinity.

ASSENS, a port of Denmark, on the W coast of the island of Funen, on the Little Belt, in N lat. 55° 17', E long. 9° 54', 22 m. WSW of Odensee. Pop. 1,700. It is the ordinary port of embarkation for the coast of Sleswick.
ASSERAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inférieure, cant. of Herbignac. Pop, 1,742.
ASSERIGO, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo-Ultra Secunda, 8 m. NE of Aquila. Pop. 642.

ASSES' EARS, two small rocky islets in the Eastern sea, near the coast of Kiusiu, in N lat. 32° 2½, E long. 128° 36½. They are about a mile apart.—Also an islet in the Chinese sea, in E long. 114° 7', N lat. 21° 55'.—Also two pinnacles on a mountain-ridge near Ras Sharwin, in Arabia, at the entrance of the Red sea.
ASSESSE, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. and 9 m. SE of Namur. Pop. 1,154. Fine marble is quarried here.
ASSEY, or ATHSY, a parish in co. Meath, 4 m. S of Navan, on the Boyne. Area 1,218 acres. Pop. 138.
ASSHRUF. See ASHRUFF.
ASSIN, a small state in Guinea, tributary to the king of Ashantee. It lies to the E of the Bussumpra river, and N of the Fantee territory.
ASSINA, a town of Western Africa, in Sudan, 300 m. NE of Whidah, in N lat. 9° 16'.
ASSINA'RA, a town of Western Africa, 3 hours N of Afura. It is surrounded by a low mud wall, and a shallow ditch.

ASSINEE, a river of Guinea, formed by the junction of the Tando from the NE and the Bara from the NW, and flowing to the W of S, through the Aowin territory, to the Atlantic, into which it falls 81 m. WNW of Cape Three Points.
ASSINGTON, a parish in Suffolk, 3 m. N of Nayland. Area 3,450 acres. Pop. 778.

ASSINIBOINE, or Assinaboyn [Franklin], a large river of British North America, which has its source in the average lat. of 52°, long. 103°, and flows to the E of S, and then SE to its junction with the Mouse, a little under the 50th parallel, when it turns E, and joins the Red river on the l. bank, at a point about 30 m. above the influx of the united rivers into the S extremity of Lake Winnipeg. Both the A. and the Red river are navigable by canoes to near their source. The total length of course of the A. is about 350 m.

ASSINIBOINS, or STONE INDIANS, a tribe of Sioux, who speak a dialect of the Iroquois. They inhabit the country watered by the above river; and with their neighbours, the Crees, maintain a constant warfare against the tribes to the westward.

ASSISI, a city of Italy, in the States-of-the-Church, 13 m. ESE of Perugia. Pop. in 1833, 5,981. It is a neat, well-built place, stretching across the sides of a hill. Its long rows of aqueducts, its old battlemented walls, and its lofty commanding citadel, combine with the site to render it a very striking and picturesque object. St. Francis, the founder of the Franciscan order, was born here in 1182; and his ashes repose in the lower subterranean church or vault of the conventual church. A. was also the birth-place of Metastasio. It has some manufactories of needles and iron files; and a great fair is held here from the 21st of July to the 1st of August.
ASSLING, a town of Austria, in Carniola, on the 1. bank of the Save, 15 m. SW of Klagenfurt. Its Slavonian name is JESENITZE.
ASSOMPTION. See Assumption.
ASSON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées, cant. of Nay. Pop. 2,582.

ASSOS, a village of Turkey, on the coast of the gulf of Adramyti, 12 m. E of Cape Baba, more commonly known by its modern name of Bearam. The remains of the ancient Assos still exist here in a remarkable state of preservation. Not only the theatre, with its seats and proscenium, but even baths, temples, and tombs, remain comparatively entire. The acropolis commands a fine view of the island of Mitylene. ASSUAN, ASOUAN [Wilkinson], Es-Souan [St. John], As-Souan [Champollion], a town of Upper Egypt, the ancient Syene, in N lat. 24° 5½, opposite the island of Elephantina, now called Jezirat-Assuan, or 'the Island of Assuan.' It is a long straggling town, with a small bazaar, on the slope of a barren hill. It exports dates, senna, henneh, wicker-baskets, and slaves from Abyssinia and Upper Nubia. The environs are sandy and barren. The Nile has here the appearance of a narrow lake, surrounded on all sides by arid picturesque rocks, mostly of granite, with some syenite, and a little porphyry. A little to the S are the remains of the ancient Saracen town, of the importance of which some idea may be formed from the fact that, according to Makrizi, 21,000 of its pop. died of the plague in the year 806 of the Mahommedan era.—The distance from Cairo to A., which is the last town in Upper Egypt previous to AST crossing the Nubian frontier, is 545 m.; and this distance was performed by a steamer of 24 horse-power, of great length, but drawing only 2 ft. 6 in. water, in the latter end of October, while the inundation of the river was still high, in 116 hours actual steaming, or at the rate of about 5 knots an hour. The descent was accomplished in 52\frac{1}{2} hours.
ASSUDA, a town in the Sahara, 135 m. N of Aghadez, in the kingdom of Asben.
ASSUR, a town of Nubia, in Dongola, 12 m. NE of Shendy, on the r. bank of the Nile.

ASSUAPMOUSSOIN, a lake in Lower Canada, in Saguenay co., 10 m. in length, and 6 m. broad, in N lat. 49° 27', W long. 73° 55'. Several smaller lakes discharge themselves into it; and it is itself connected with a large river of the same name, which falls into the W side of Lake St. John, after a general course from NW to SE. It is nearly 3 of a mile wide at its mouth, and some distance above it nearly 3 m. Its navigation is interrupted by numerous falls.
ASSUAY, a prov. and town of Ecuador, on the E side of the gulf of Guayaquil.

ASSUMPÇAO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Guayana, on the l. bank of the Putomayo.—Also a town in the prov. of Goyaz, on the l. bank of the Toccantin.—Also a river in the prov. of Seguro, flowing into the Atlantic N of Cape Frio. - Also a river in the prov. of Minas Geraes, a branch of the Rio Verde.

ASSUMPÇÃO (NUESTRA SENHORA D'), or more briefly Assumption, the capital of Paraguay, on the 1. bank of the Paraguay river, in S lat. 25° 16', W long. 57° 37′, 18 m. above the first mouth of the Pilcomayo. It was founded in 1535. Little is known of its present condition; but it is believed to contain not more than 400 or 500 families. The neighbourhood is well-cultivated and fertile, producing wheat, maize, mandioc, sugar, cotton, and tobacco. While it had any commercial intercourse with the neighbouring states, A. exported considerable quantities of hides, tobacco, and sugar; and the dried leaves of an herb called matte, or 'Paraguay tea,' which is a favourite substitute for the Chinese leaf throughout Chili and Peru. Large boats navigate the Paraguay from Buenos Ayres to A., in from 9 to 10 weeks, if aided by a favourable wind.
ASSUMPCION, a town of Brazil, in the prov. and 37 m. N of Pernambuco.—Also a town of Ecnador, on the l. bank of the Putomayo.

ASSUMPTION, one of the Ladrone group of islands, in the Pacific ocean, in N lat. 19° 45', E long. 145° 54'. It is of a conical figure; about 3 m. in circumf.; and rises to the height of 600 ft. It is covered with volcanic products; but produces cocoanuts, rice, oranges, and the bread-fruit.—Also a small uninhabited island in the Indian ocean, S lat. 9° 44', E long. 46° 40′, a dependency of the Mauritius.—Also a parish in the SE part of Louisiana, U.S., watered by the Bayou la Fourche. Pop. in 1840, 7,141. Its cap. of the same name is 89 m. W of New Orleans.
ASSUMSTADT, a town of Baden, 5 m. SSE of Boxberg. Pop. 1,000. It is celebrated for its fruit.
ASSUNGIOVA, a town of Turkey in Europe, 32 m. W of Adrianople, on the Assum river.
ASSYE. See Assaye.

ASSYNT, a very extensive parish in the NW of Sutherlandshire, its extreme length being 36 m., and its area being estimated at 97,000 acres. Its northern coast is formed by the channel called the Minch and by Loch Assynt. Pop. in 1801, 2,395; in 1841, 3,178. Benmore, or Conval, in the S part of this p., has an alt. of 3,230 ft. above sea-level.

ASTABAT, a town of Russian Armenia, 20 m. SE of Nakshivan, on the river Aras. It is a small but neat place. In the neighbourhood the inhabitants cultivate great quantities of ronas, the roots of which are used in dyeing red.

ASTAFFORT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, arrond. of Agen, on the Gers. Pop. of com., 2,785. ASTAHBONA'T—pronounced Savonat—a large village of Persia, in Farsistan, 16 m. SE of Kheir, and 60 m. NW of Darabgerd. It is a thriving town, and possesses some manufactures of linen, fine earthenware, and wood-ware. - Ouseley.
ASTANE, a town of Chinese Tartary, in the prov. of Hami, 20 m. NW of Hami, in N lat. 42° 2'.

ASTARAH, a river of Persia, which runs into the Caspian sea, in lat. 38° 35' N, forming the N boundary of the prov. of Ghilan, which it separates from Russian Persia. There is a town of the same name, situated on each side of the stream a quarter of a mile above its mouth. It is 70 m. N of Reshd, and 100 E of Tabriz.
ASTBURY, a parish in Cheshire, 2 m. S of Congleton. Area 18,070 acres. Pop. 3,408.
ASTELL'S ISLAND, an island of Australia, in the NW part of the gulf of Carpentaria, near Cape Arnheim. It is of moderate height, and wooded.
ASTEN, a village of Holland, in N Brabant, 13 m. SE of Eindhoven. Pop. 2,280.

ASTENBERG, a village of Prussia, in Westphalia, in the circle of Arnsberg, to the E of Brilon. It has an elevation of 2,854 ft. above sea-level, and is on the summit-level between the basins of the Weser and the Rhine.
ASTENE, a commune of Belgium, in E Flanders, 8 m. W by S of Ghent, on the Lys. Pop. 1,351.

ASTERABAD, a small prov. of Persia, sometimes included in that of Mazanderan, which it resembles in climate and productions. It is bounded on the W by the Caspian; on the S by a lofty ridge of mountains separating it from the districts of Damgan and Bistan; on the N by the river Ashor, which divides it from Daghistan; on the E it extends as far as 58° W long. It is nearly identical with the ancient Hyrcania; and is the paternal estate of the king of Persia, as chief of the tribe Kajar, or Kujur, which has entire possession of it. The revenue derived from it is about £7,000.—Kinneir.—Fraser.

ASTERA'BA'D, a town of Persia, the cap. of the above prov., on the SE shore of the Caspian, near the mouth of the river Aster or Easter, in N lat. 36° 51', E long. 54° 25' 33" [Fraser], 300 m. NNE of Ispahan. It was once a trading-place of some consequence; but at present the river which washes its walls has a course of several miles to the W before it reaches the sea; and the bay is much choked-up with sand. [Eichwald.] The circuit of the present city is about 35 m. All the streets are well-paved with stone, and the bazaars are extensive; but a large proportion of it, notwithstanding, is in ruins; and the thicket and forest here threaten to invade the abodes of men, for the bramble and thorn, the wild pomegranate and plum, form an impenetrable cover up to the very walls of the place. [Fraser.] By the magnificent causeway of Shah Abbas, which is still in tolerable repair, A. keeps up communication with the provinces S of the Caspian. Its trade with Khiva employs one or two annual caravans of 80 or 100 camels. [Burnes.] Hanway, an English merchant, attempted to open a direct trade with this place, but in the course of a rebellion which broke out against Nadir Shah, in 1743 and 1744, his property was plundered, and he narrowly escaped being sold for a slave. On applying, however, to Nadir, he was ordered full indemnification from the royal treasury. A. was founded about the beginning of the second cent. of the Hegira. It was destroyed by Tamerlane; it is now governed by a descendant of the reigning family of Persia. The dis- Ludlow. Area 1,090 acres. Pop. 52.—Also a parish AST tance from this point of Central Asia to Delhi is 1,717 m.; or, allowing for the deviation of roads, about 2,200 m.
ASTERWEIN, a town of Prussia, in the Oberland, 6 m. E of Osterrod.
ASTERY, a river of Sussex, falling into the sea at Hastings, after a short course.
ASTFELD, a village of Brunswick, at the foot of the Hartz, 2 m. NW of Goslar. Pop. 500.
ASTHALL, a parish in Oxfordshire, 3 m. E by S of Burford. Area 1,180 acres. Pop. 389.
ASTHEIM, a village of Hesse-Darmstadt, near the r. bank of the Rhine, 14 m. NW of Darmstadt. Pop. 650.—Also a village of Bavaria, on the Maine.
ASTHOLM, an island in the gulf of Bothnia, in N lat. 62° 25′, E long. 17° 33′.

ASTI, a district of Sardinia, one of the intendenze or subdivisions of Alessandria; bounded on the W and N by Turin; on the NE by Casale; on the SE by Alessandria Proper; and on the S by Alba. It is about 25 m. long, and 10 broad; and forms part of the dominions of the house of Savoy, into whose possession it came in 1531. It is a hilly, but fruitful and populous territory, watered by the Tanaro. It produces excellent silk and wines, and abounds in mineral and thermal springs. It is subdivided into 87 communes. Its pop. in 1826 was 118,709. Astr, the capital of the above, lies on the left bank of the Tanaro, on the high-road from Turin to Alessandria, 26 m. distant by road from the latter place, in N lat. 44° 57', E long. 80° 12'. Its pop. in 1825 was 22,000. It is a large city, and well-built; and is the see of a bishop, a suffragan of the archbishop of Turin. It conducts a considerable trade in corn, wine, and silk, which is promoted by the situation of the town on the high-road, a circumstance, however, which exposed it to heavy losses in the great military movements in 1799, particularly in the retreat of the French under Moreau, and in the advance of the Russians to Turin. A. was the birthplace of the poet Alfieri on 17th January 1749. During the middle ages, it attained considerable importance as an independent republic. In the 14th cent. it formed a part of the territory of the lords of Milan, and was transferred by marriage to the duke of Orleans. In 1529 it was ceded to the emperor Charles V., who made it over to Emmanuel of Savoy in 1531.
ASTIER (SAINT), a canton and commune of France, dep. of Dordogne, arrond. and 10 m. WSW of Perigueux. Pop. of com. 2,546.
ASTIGORRAGA, a town of Spain, in Guipuscoa, in the prov. and 4 m. SSE of St. Sebastian, on the r. bank of the Urumea.

ASTLEY, a chapelry in the p. of Leigh, Lancashire, 5 m. ENE of Newton. Area 2,020 acres. Pop. 2,011.—Also a chapelry in Salop, in the p. of St. Mary, Shrewsbury. Pop. 264.—Also a parish in Warwick, 41 m. WSW of Nuneaton. Area 2,550 acres. Pop. 371.—Also a parish in Worcestershire, 5 m. S of Bewdley. Area 3,310 acres. Pop. 834.
ASTLEY-ABBOTS, a parish in Salop, 2 m. N of Bridgnorth. Area 3,330 acres. Pop. 657.

ASTON, a hamlet of Bucks, in the p. of Ivinghoe, 1 m. NNE of Ivinghoe. Pop. 446.—Also a township in the p. of Great Budworth, Cheshire, 3 m. N by E of Northwich. Area 2,830 acres. Pop. 405.—Also a township in the p. of Runcorn, 3 m. E by N of Frodsham. Area 550 acres. Pop. 27.—Also a p. in Derbyshire, 63 m. NNE of Tideswell. Pop. 111. -Also a township in the parish of Hawarden, Flintshire, 11 m. N by W of Hawarden. Pop. 303. Extensive iron-works employ the chief part of the inhabitants.-Also a parish in Hereford, 4 m. SW of in Hertford, 3 m. SE of Stevenage. Area 1,990 acres. Pop. 556.-A township in Montgomeryshire, in the parish of Lydham, 3 m. W of Church-Stretton. -A hamlet in Bampton parish, Oxfordshire, 5 m. SSW of Witney. Area 1,870 acres. Pop. 523.—A township in the parish of Muckleston, Staffordshire, 4 m. NE of Drayton-on-Hales. Area 910 acres. Pop. 259.—A parish in Warwickshire, 2 m. N by E of Birmingham. Area 13,330 acres. Pop. 45,718, chiefly artizans employed in the manufactories of Birmingham.-A parish, with Aughton, in the W R. of York, 6 m. ESE of Rotheram. Area 2,870 acres. Pop. 763.
ASTON-ABBOTTS, a parish in Bucks, 5 m. NNE of Aylesbury. Area 2,180 acres. Pop. 356.
ASTON-BLANK, a parish in Gloucestershire, 4 m. N by E of North Leach. Area 2,250 acres. Pop. 302.
ASTON-BOTTERELL, a parish in Salop, 81 m. SW of Bridgnorth. Area 3,000 acres. Pop. 173.
ASTON-CANTLOW, a parish in Warwickshire, 4 m. NE of Alcester. Area 4,300 acres. Pop. 1,089.
ASTON-BY-SUTTON, a chapelry in the parish of Runcorn, Cheshire. Area 1,490 acres. Pop. 206.
ASTON-CHETWYND, a township in the parish of Edgmond, in Salop. Pop. 385.
ASTON-CHURCH, or Church-Aston, a chapelry in the parish of Edgmond, Salop. Pop. 512.
ASTON-CLINTON, a parish in Bucks, 3 m. W by N of Tring. Area 3,640 acres. Pop. 1,025.
ASTON-COAL, a township in the parish of Dronfield, Derbyshire. Pop. 352.
ASTON-COLD, a parish in Gloucestershire, in the hund. of Bradley. Area 2,250 acres. Pop. 302.
ASTON-EYRE, a chapelry in the parish of Morvill, Salop, 3 m. WNW of Bridgnorth. Acres 1,330. Pop. 130.
ASTON-FLAMVILLE, a parish in Leicestershire, 3 m. ESE of Hinckley. Acres 4,670. Pop. 1,909.
ASTON-GRANGE, a township in Chester, in the p. of Runcorn, 4 m. E of Frodsham. Acres 550. Pop. 27.
ASTON-INGHAM, a parish in Herefordshire, 6 m. E by S of Ross. Acres 2,300. Pop. 621.
ASTON-MIDDLE, a township in the parish of Aston-Steeple, Oxfordshire, 3 m. S by E of Deddington. Pop. 111.
ASTON-JUXTA-MONDRUM, a township in the parish of Acton, Cheshire, 4 m. N of Nantwich. Acres 950. Pop. 164.
